summary: Jeff Atwood critiques John Gruber's stewardship of Markdown, arguing that despite creating an excellent markup language, Gruber has abandoned his responsibilities as its maintainer. The post details specific Markdown bugs and design issues discovered through Stack Overflow's real-world usage, noting that fixes exist but were never officially released. Atwood argues that Gruber's refusal to collaborate with the community, host the project on a collaborative platform, or release updates has fragmented the ecosystem and stunted Markdown's growth. The core argument is that open source creators have ongoing responsibilities to their projects, and neglecting these duties harms both the project and the community that depends on it.
key_points: Markdown has fundamental design issues (auto-hyperlinking URLs, underscore handling, linebreak behavior) that Stack Overflow and GitHub both had to independently fix
Gruber has unreleased bugfixes (1.0.2 betas) and no official test suite, forcing implementers to hunt down unofficial resources
The project's static website hosting prevents community collaboration that platforms like SourceForge or GitHub would enable
Gruber's ego-driven approach to running the project mirrors Steve Jobs at Apple, blocking community contributions rather than embracing them
Open source projects need active parenting—without leadership, you get fragmented community efforts with no canonical direction
money_quotes: Markdown is the worst form of markup except for all the other forms of markup that I've tried.
Which leads me to the biggest problem with Markdown: John Gruber.
John is running this particular open source project the way Steve Jobs runs Apple – by sheer force of individual ego. And that sucks.
It's almost at the point where John Gruber, the very person who brought us Markdown, is the biggest obstacle preventing Markdown from moving forward and maturing.
Maybe it's true that you can't kill an open source project, but bad parenting is surely enough to cause it to grow up stunted and maybe even a little maladjusted.
Why work against the community when you can work with it?
Markdown is Gruber's baby, without a doubt, but it's also bigger than any one person. It's open source. It belongs to the community, too.
key_insight: Creating successful open source software comes with ongoing responsibilities to the community, and neglecting those duties—even for your own creation—can make you the biggest obstacle to its success.
